serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of pan-fried XO radish cake is approximately 200 g, comprising several golden-brown pan-fried slices of steamed radish cake tossed with XO sauce, egg, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Carrot cake (chai tow kway), fried — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists fried chai tow kway (carrot cake) as the closest approved match. The XO sauce variant adds dried seafood and additional fat; values are adapted upward for fat and sodium to reflect XO sauce contribution. Core macros anchored to HPB fried chai tow kway baseline. Secondary cross-reference: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for rice flour and daikon composition to validate carbohydrate and fibre estimates.

gi_source.title: Rice flour-based steamed and pan-fried cake — analogous estimate from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for XO radish cake or chai tow kway. GI estimated at 52 based on rice flour-based steamed cake analogues (GI range 48–56 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables). Pan-frying and the presence of protein and fat from egg and XO sauce further attenuate glycemic response. GL per serving = (52 × 23.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 24.4 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from XO sauce, soy sauce, dried seafood, and Chinese sausage
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid pan-fried XO radish cake due to its high sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1040 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents nearly half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from rice flour base with low-to-moderate GI attenuated by fat and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume pan-fried XO radish cake in small portions, as the rice flour base contributes a moderate glycemic load (GL approximately 24 per 200 g serving) despite a low estimated GI of 52; the added fat and protein from XO sauce and egg help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content including saturated fat from Chinese sausage and XO sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of pan-fried XO radish cake due to its elevated sodium content and moderate saturated fat contribution from Chinese sausage and XO sauce, both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pan-fried XO Radish Cake, known in Cantonese as Lo Bak Go and in Singaporean Hokkien as Chai Tow Kway, is a popular Chinese dim sum and hawker dish widely enjoyed in Singapore and Hong Kong. The dish is made from a steamed cake of rice flour and grated white radish (daikon), which is then sliced and pan-fried until golden brown and crispy on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. The XO variant elevates the classic preparation by tossing the fried cake pieces with XO sauce — a premium Cantonese condiment made from dried scallops, dried shrimp, Jinhua ham, and chilli — along with egg, spring onion, and soy sauce. The result is a savoury, umami-rich dish with a complex seafood aroma, a slightly smoky wok fragrance, and a satisfying contrast of textures. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (approximately 185 kcal per 100 g) and provides carbohydrates primarily from rice flour, with meaningful protein from egg and dried seafood. The sodium content is notably high due to XO sauce, soy sauce, and dried seafood, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (approximately 52), moderated by the fat and protein content. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish, egg, or soy allergies. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served as a dim sum item or hawker centre snack, and the XO version is considered a premium upgrade over the standard white or black (with dark soy sauce) versions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pan-fried radish cake?
What is XO sauce made of?

Can diabetics eat chai tow kway?
What is the difference between white and black carrot cake in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for XO radish cake specifically. Values are adapted from the HPB fried chai tow kway (carrot cake) baseline with upward adjustment for fat and sodium to reflect XO sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from rice flour-based cake data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the HPB baseline is a strong approved primary source for the core dish, and the XO sauce adaptation is well-supported by ingredient composition data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried chai tow kway baseline. The XO sauce variant is not separately listed in HPB; fat and sodium values have been adjusted upward to reflect the XO sauce, dried seafood, and Chinese sausage contribution. 2. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on rice flour steamed cake data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables; no direct measurement for chai tow kway or XO radish cake was found. GL per 200 g serving = (52 × 23.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 24.4. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this food category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this food category. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this food category. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this food category. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this food category. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this food category. 9. Halal status is uncertain as XO sauce traditionally contains pork-derived ingredients (Jinhua ham); restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified. 10. The food_group is retained as Desserts per the input record, consistent with the field survey categorisation, though this dish is more accurately a savoury snack or dim sum item in culinary terms.
